name: Word Problems — Choosing the Right Operation
whatIsIt: A word problem is a story hiding a calculation. The hard part is not the arithmetic — it is choosing WHICH operation the story needs: joining (+), taking away or comparing (−), equal groups (×), or sharing/grouping (÷). The bar model turns the story into a picture, and the picture reveals the operation.
whyNeeded: Real life never says 'compute 52 − 27'. It says 'you had 52 rupees and bought a toy…'. Every exam, every shop, every game speaks in stories. A child who can turn stories into math can solve ANYTHING — this is the skill all ten earlier concepts were building toward.
realLifeUses: Shop stories: money paid, cost, change
Cricket stories: runs scored, runs needed, run rates
Kitchen stories: laddus made, shared, left over
Travel stories: distance covered and remaining

vocabulary.meaning: Read → Extract → Arrange (draw!) → Select operation → Operate → Notice (check).
Rectangles that picture the story: parts, wholes, and comparisons.
What the story DOES: joins, removes, compares, groups equally, or shares.

story.title: The Sweet Tray at the Wedding
story.text: At a village wedding, the halwai's big tray holds 6 rows of 8 laddus. Hungry guests eat 15 of them. 'How many are left?' Meena thinks in two steps: 'Equal rows mean MULTIPLY — 6 × 8 = 48 laddus to start. Eaten means SUBTRACT — 48 − 15 = 33. Read the words, then pick the right step!'
story.extractedProblem: A box has 6 rows of 8 laddus; 15 are eaten. How many laddus are left?
story.answerInStory: 33 — equal rows say multiply (6×8=48), 'eaten' says subtract (48−15=33).

visual.caption: Bar 1: six equal parts of 8 → multiply. Bar 2: the whole splits into 'left' and 'eaten' → subtract. Pictures choose operations!

teachingGallery.title: Which operation? Read the story
teachingGallery.note: Keywords tell you what to do: altogether=+, left=−, each/groups=×, share=÷.

standardMethod.summary: R.E.A.S.O.N. through every story — never guess the operation.
steps: READ and restate: what is the story asking?
EXTRACT: what do we know? What is unknown?
ARRANGE: draw a bar model of the story.
SELECT: joining→+, removing/comparing→−, equal groups→×, sharing/grouping→÷.
OPERATE step by step, then NOTICE: does the answer make sense in the story?

alternateMethods.name: Operation-clue spotting
alternateMethods.whenToUse: A quick first instinct before drawing.
steps: JOIN words (altogether, total, in all, combined) hint +.
REMOVE/COMPARE words (left, remain, change, more than, fewer) hint −.
EQUAL-GROUP words (each, every, per, rows of) hint × …or ÷!
'Each' with the TOTAL known means ÷; 'each' with the total UNKNOWN means ×. The bar model settles any doubt.
alternateMethods.example: '5 boxes of 12' → total unknown → ×. '60 laddus, 12 per box' → total known → ÷.

alternateMethods.name: Answer-sense check
steps: Put your answer back into the story: does it sound possible?
Left-over answers must be SMALLER than the start.
Sharing answers must be smaller than the total; totals must be bigger than each part.
alternateMethods.example: '52 − 27 = 35 laddus left'? But 35 + 27 ≠ 52 — the story itself rejects it!

workedExamples.problem: Anu read 38 pages on Saturday and 26 on Sunday. How many pages altogether?
steps: What happens? Two amounts JOIN into one total.
Bar: two parts (38 and 26) making one whole.
Joining → ADD: 38 + 26 = 64.
Sense check: 64 is bigger than both parts ✓

workedExamples.problem: 60 laddus are packed 12 to a box. Ravi shouts 'each means MULTIPLY — 720 laddus!' Untangle him.
steps: What happens? A TOTAL (60) is being broken into equal groups of 12.
The total is already KNOWN — nothing new is being built.
Breaking a known total into equal groups → DIVIDE: 60 ÷ 12 = 5 boxes.
Sense check: 720 boxes from 60 laddus?! The story laughs. 5 boxes ✓

trickPractice.intro: Let the words pick the sign: altogether +, left −, each/groups ×, share ÷.

questions.q: Riya has 4 mangoes. Papa gives 3 more. How many ALTOGETHER?
A shop had 12 laddus and sold 5. How many are LEFT?
There are 5 baskets with 4 apples EACH. How many apples?
Share 20 sweets equally among 4 friends. How many EACH?
hintLadder: 'Share equally' means split into equal groups.

questions.q: A pack has 6 pens. You buy 3 packs. How many pens?
28 children, 13 go home. How many STILL there?

commonMistakes.mistake: Grabbing the two numbers and guessing an operation without reading what happens.
commonMistakes.fix: Numbers don't choose the operation — the STORY's action does. Ask: is something joining, leaving, grouping, or sharing? Draw the bar first, calculate second.

commonMistakes.mistake: Treating 'each/every' as an automatic multiply — even when the total is already given.
commonMistakes.fix: 'Each' has two faces! Total unknown → × builds it. Total already known → ÷ breaks it. Check which piece of the bar is missing.

commonMistakes.mistake: Accepting any answer without asking if it makes sense in the story (720 boxes from 60 laddus!).
commonMistakes.fix: Always finish with the N of R.E.A.S.O.N. — NOTICE. Put the answer back in the story: leftovers smaller than the start, shares smaller than the total, totals bigger than parts.

easy.q: 'Anu has 24 marbles. Ravi gives her 13 more. How many now?' Which operation?
hintLadder: What HAPPENS in the story — joining, leaving, grouping, or sharing?
'Gives her more' — amounts are coming together.

easy.q: A bus had 45 passengers; 18 got down at the market. How many are still on the bus?
hintLadder: What happens? People LEAVE the bus.
Borrow if you need to! Check: your answer + 18 = 45?

medium.q: A parking lot has 7 rows with 15 cars in each row. How many cars in all?
hintLadder: 'Each row' and the total is UNKNOWN — which face of 'each' is this?
Building a total from equal groups → multiply.

medium.q: 72 students travel 9 to a van. How many vans are needed?
hintLadder: '9 to a van' and the total (72) is KNOWN — which face of 'each'?
Breaking a known total into equal groups → divide.

challenge.q: Two steps! A tray has 4 rows of 6 laddus. 9 laddus are eaten. How many are left?
hintLadder: Step 1: equal rows build the total — 4 × 6 = ?
Sense check: is your answer smaller than 24?

challenge.q: 'Anu is 132 cm tall. Ravi is 9 cm shorter.' Ravi writes 132 + 9 = 141 cm for his own height. What went wrong?
hintLadder: Draw two bars: Anu's, and Ravi's which is 9 SHORTER.
Is Ravi's bar longer or shorter than Anu's?
Shorter bar = smaller number = subtract. And sense-check: can 'shorter' make him taller?!

questions.q: '56 mangoes are shared equally among 8 friends.' Which operation?

options.label: Divide — a known total is being shared

questions.answer: Divide — a known total is being shared
hintLadder: Is the total known or being built?

questions.q: A shop sold 34 kites in the morning and 48 in the evening. Total kites sold?
hintLadder: Two amounts joining into one day's total.

questions.q: Two steps: 5 packets of 20 biscuits; 27 are eaten at the party. How many left?
The FINAL step of every word problem, per Robo Reason, is…

options.label: NOTICE: put the answer back in the story — does it make sense?
Move on quickly to the next question

questions.answer: NOTICE: put the answer back in the story — does it make sense?
hintLadder: Leftovers smaller than the start, totals bigger than parts.

questions.q: You pay ₹90 for 6 identical notebooks. What does ONE notebook cost in rupees?
hintLadder: Total known (90), equal groups (6 notebooks) — which operation?

teachBackPrompt: Become the Story Untangler! Make up ONE word problem of your own about your home (food, cricket, money — anything), draw its bar model, and explain to someone which operation the story chose and why.

revisionCard.summary: Stories choose operations, not luck! R.E.A.S.O.N.: Read → Extract → Arrange (draw the bar!) → Select (+ join, − remove/compare, × build equal groups, ÷ break a known total) → Operate → NOTICE (sense-check!). 'Each' has two faces: total unknown → ×, total known → ÷.

gameMission.title: The Story Untangler
gameMission.brief: Tangled stories are piling up at Robo's desk! Draw the right bar, pick the true operation, and sense-check every answer. Beware Ravi's favourite trap: shouting 'MULTIPLY!' at every 'each'.

realLifeProject: Story-hunt at dinner: each family member tells one real number story from their day ('I bought…', 'we walked…'). You untangle each one out loud: what's known, what's asked, which operation, and the answer — sense-checked!

meta.curriculum: CBSE/NCERT Class 4 (word problems, all four operations)

mentalMathMethod.name: Pólya's four steps
steps: UNDERSTAND: what is given, what is asked?
PLAN: which operation fits — join, remove, groups, share?
SOLVE it, then CHECK the answer makes sense.
